candidates.text: Being on-call shouldn't mean being constantly interrupted. If you're getting paged more than twice a week, something is fundamentally broken in your system or your monitoring. How often do you get paged?

New Product Idea:

"pagerduty but for europeans"

If there is an incident on the weekend, it waits until monday to tell you

First day and you shipped auth to prod?
Coffee is the rollback plan.
I've had my fair share of late nights trying to perfect my app's UI, only to be woken up by an email saying the server went down while I slept, and I'm left scrambling to fix it at 3 am.
Nothing like waking up to PagerDuty Alerts because an AWS region has been blown up. I can say this is definitely a first for me

Backend engineering lessons nobody teaches in tutorials:

Real shit from production.

1/10
Your production bugs happen at Mid-night on weekends.

Never during work hours.
Never when you're prepared.

Always when you're asleep or at a wedding.

Murphy's law is real.

The real bottleneck is always the cost vs. risk analysis. Maintaining a warm standby in a second region can double your AWS bill instantly. Most startups pray for the best, but for enterprise apps, if you haven't tested a regional failover, you don't actually have one

If your Vercel deployments are failing with "We encountered an internal error" right now — it's not your code.
Vercel is experiencing elevated deployment & function failures (started ~06:00 UTC, Mar 2).
Build succeeds → "Deploying outputs..." fails. Redeploying doesn't help.
